text.title: Design Critique Process and Roles
text.summary: Critique vs. criticism distinction, session roles, stage-appropriate feedback types, and five-step critique process.

section.title: Critique vs. Criticism
section.p: Critique: based on stated goals, specific and actionable, about the work, asks questions, improves design. Criticism: based on personal preference, vague, about the designer, makes judgments, hurts feelings.

rule.statement: Every piece of feedback must be connected to a stated design goal or usability principle.
rule.rationale: Feedback not anchored to a goal is preference; preference-based feedback cannot be evaluated objectively and degrades into opinion battles.

section.title: Roles
section.p: Presenter — shows work, states goals and constraints, asks for specific feedback type. Facilitator — keeps discussion on track, ensures all voices are heard, redirects opinion to goals. Participants — ask clarifying questions before giving feedback, connect observations to goals. Note-taker — captures key feedback, questions raised, decisions made, action items.

rule.statement: The presenter decides what feedback to incorporate; it is their design, their call.
rule.rationale: Removing designer agency from critique sessions destroys ownership and produces resentful implementation; the presenter's authority over their work must be explicit.

section.title: Stage-Appropriate Feedback
section.p: Directional feedback (Is this the right approach?) — for exploration stage; many options open. Refinement feedback (How can this be better?) — for iteration stage; one direction being developed. Polish feedback (Is this ready to ship?) — for final stage; micro-decisions and completeness.

rule.statement: State the design stage before showing designs; polish feedback on exploration work is harmful.
rule.rationale: Polish feedback on early exploration locks in a direction prematurely and wastes time debating details that may be discarded.

section.title: Five-Step Process
section.p: Step 1: Set context — problem, user, constraints, stage, feedback type requested.
Step 2: Present the work systematically — explain decisions made, point out areas of uncertainty, do not over-apologize.
Step 3: Clarifying questions — participants ask to understand, not judge, before giving feedback.
Step 4: Give structured feedback — Observation → Principle/Goal → Impact → (Optional suggestion).
Step 5: Capture and prioritize — note-taker documents feedback, questions, decisions, and action items.

example.note: Observation: "The submit button is below the fold." Principle: "Users frequently miss below-fold CTAs on mobile." Impact: "This could hurt conversion on small screens." Suggestion: "What if we added a sticky footer button?"
"I don't like the colors." — preference statement with no observation, no principle, no impact. Not actionable.

text.reference: Observation → Principle → Impact format with worked example
